frontmatter.title: Introduction to NSF NCAR’s Geoscience Data Exchange

affiliations.name: NSF National Center for Atmospheric Research

children.value: is the long-term, trusted home for geoscience data at the

. GDEX bridges the gap between data producers and the broader scientific community by providing curated, openly accessible datasets alongside the tools and services that can help enable scientific insight.

Whether you are looking to download terabytes of reanalysis data, stream a climate model output directly into a Python workflow, or archive your own research dataset with a citable DOI, GDEX is built to support the full lifecycle of geoscience data.
In this Cookbook you will learn:

children.label: In this Cookbook you will learn:

children.value: The mission and purpose of GDEX
The breadth of data collections available
Key services and tools for data access and analysis
How to submit your own data to GDEX
How GDEX enables reproducible, open science
GDEX exists to  make geoscience data
to the NCAR scientists and the broader community.

GDEX is committed to open science. Currently, all datasets are freely accessible to anyone, anywhere in the world without institutional or technical barriers. Additionally, data services are continuously being developed and expanded to minimize friction with obtaining and interacting with data.
GDEX supports data from initial archival through active use in research workflows:
: Data submitted to GDEX is reviewed, documented, and formatted to community standards
: Rich metadata and search tools help users find the right dataset quickly
: Multiple access pathways — from bulk download to cloud streaming
: Persistent identifiers (DOIs) ensure datasets are citable and contributions are credited
: GDEX provides long-term stewardship and data backups
: Science-educated staff assist users in solving problems and forwarding science.
All GDEX datasets are freely accessible — no institutional login is required to browse, search, or download public datasets.
GDEX hosts thousands of datasets spanning the full breadth of the geosciences. Collections range from petabytes of global reanalysis output to niche observational records from field campaigns.
— the International Comprehensive Ocean-Atmosphere Data Set, spanning centuries of marine surface observations
: Soil moisture, vegetation, and land-use datasets supporting land-atmosphere interaction research
: Sea ice concentration, snow cover, and ice sheet data
GDEX datasets are available in community-standard formats, increasingly including cloud-optimized options:
NWP model output from operational centers
Virtual consolidated metadata for remote access
GDEX offers a comprehensive suite of services to help researchers at every stage of their workflow.
provides powerful tools for finding data:
across thousands of datasets with filtering by variable, domain, date range, and format
including dataset documentation, version history, and related publications
and quick-look visualizations to assess data before downloading
GDEX meets researchers wherever they work:
Browsing and ad-hoc downloads via the portal
Bulk, reliable transfer of large datasets to HPC systems
Programmatic and subsetting access from scripts
Integration directly into data science workflows
Many datasets support server-side subsetting, allowing you to request only the variables, spatial domain, and time range you need — reducing download size and time significantly.
For large datasets where moving data is impractical, GDEX data is accessible from NSF NCAR computing resources including the
analysis cluster. Researchers with allocations can run analyses directly alongside the data.
Dataset-level metadata including abstract, variables, resources, and citations
File-level metadata following community standards and uses
Documentation and software files when applicable
Version tracking so analyses can be reproduced precisely
Links to related publications and data sources
section of this cookbook has dedicated notebooks for each access pathway: TDS/OPeNDAP, Zarr streaming, the REST API, direct download, and Globus transfer.
GDEX accepts data submissions from the broader scientific community. Archiving your data with GDEX is a way to ensure it reaches the widest possible audience and remains accessible for the long term.
with NSF or other federally funded grants that require a data management plan
If you are unsure whether your dataset is a good fit, contact the GDEX team at
What Types of Data Are Accepted?

children.label: What Types of Data Are Accepted?

children.value: for atmospheric, oceanic, or related geoscience research
your data and discuss its suitability with the GDEX team
— Work with the GDEX team to ensure your data and metadata meet community standards
— GDEX supports transfer via Globus, HTTPS, or other methods depending on data volume
— The GDEX team reviews, documents, and organizes your dataset
— Your dataset is published with a persistent DOI, making it citable in publications
that can be included in publications
Long-term preservation and backups by a trusted,
Broad discoverability through GDEX search and external indexes (e.g., Google Dataset Search)
NSF and journal data sharing requirements
Access to a variety of value added
Your data becomes part of a collection used by researchers worldwide
before investing significant effort in data preparation. The GDEX team can confirm your dataset is a good fit and advise on format and metadata requirements.
GDEX is more than a file repository — it is infrastructure that enables scientific discovery at scale.

By providing stable, versioned, citable datasets, GDEX makes it possible to reproduce published analyses years after the fact. When a paper cites a GDEX dataset DOI, any researcher in the world can access the exact same data used in that study.
Some of the most scientifically important datasets are also the largest. ERA5 global reanalysis alone spans petabytes. GDEX removes the logistical barriers to working with these datasets by:
Hosting data on infrastructure optimized for high-throughput access
Providing cloud-optimized formats (Zarr, Kerchunk) that enable streaming analysis without full downloads
Supporting compute-proximate workflows on NSF NCAR HPC systems

Because GDEX curates data to common standards, it becomes easier to combine datasets — for example, comparing reanalysis products or merging model output with observations. Consistent metadata and file formats reduce the data wrangling burden and let researchers focus on science.
The scale and diversity of GDEX collections make them well-suited for emerging methods in geoscience:
applications that require large training datasets
combining atmospheric, oceanic, and land surface data
Example: Streaming Data Directly into Python

children.label: Example: Streaming Data Directly into Python

children.value: Cloud-optimized formats hosted by GDEX allow researchers to open petabyte-scale datasets with a single line of Python — no full download required:

import xarray as xr

# Open a cloud-optimized GDEX dataset
ds = xr.open_zarr("https://data.gdex.ucar.edu/d633000/e5.oper.an.sfc.zarr/e5.oper.an.sfc.100u.zarr", consolidated=True)
ds["VAR_100U"].sel(time="2020-01-01").plot()
This workflow is possible because GDEX invests in cloud-optimized formats and stable, high-bandwidth access infrastructure.
— a deeper dive into analysis-ready and AI-ready data, cloud-optimized formats, and machine learning workflows.

import xarray as xr

# Open a cloud-optimized GDEX dataset
ds = xr.open_zarr("https://data.gdex.ucar.edu/d633000/e5.oper.an.sfc.zarr/e5.oper.an.sfc.100u.zarr", consolidated=True)
ds["VAR_100U"].sel(time="2020-01-01").plot()

children.value: NSF NCAR’s Geoscience Data Exchange (GDEX) is a trusted, long-term home for geoscience data — one that serves the research community both as a data archive and as an active platform for scientific discovery.
spanning atmospheric, oceanic, and Earth system science, from century-long observational records to petabyte-scale reanalysis products
— web download, Globus, OPeNDAP, and cloud streaming — ensure data is reachable wherever researchers work
Data submission is open to the community
: archiving your data with GDEX provides a citable DOI, long-term preservation, and broad discoverability
by lowering barriers to large-scale data access, supporting reproducibility, and providing infrastructure for modern data-driven workflows
The notebooks in this cookbook demonstrate hands-on use of GDEX data, from basic access patterns to cloud-native analysis at scale.
The GDEX team provides direct support for data access, discovery, and submission questions.
The primary support channel is the
: data access problems, subsetting questions, format guidance, submission inquiries, and anything not covered by documentation
When emailing, include the dataset identifier (e.g.,
) and a description of what you were trying to do — this significantly speeds up the response.
— try broader terms or filtering by variable
I need access to a restricted dataset
Check the dataset page for access instructions, or email the help desk
My download failed or is very slow
for large transfers, or email the help desk
I want to submit data to GDEX
or the Data Submission notebook in this cookbook
I need help with a specific Python workflow
Browse the service notebooks in this cookbook
A dataset seems incorrect or has a quality issue
Email the help desk with the dataset ID and a description of the issue
— dataset-specific documentation is linked from each dataset’s landing page
— variable naming and metadata standards used across GDEX datasets
If you find an error in this cookbook or want to suggest a new example:
Open an issue or pull request on the
Contributions are welcome — see the repository README for guidelines
— for data access questions, submission inquiries, or general support
— metadata standards used by many GDEX datasets
— cloud-native array format used for streaming access
— virtual metadata for remote Grib/NetCDF access
